title: America’s Sexual Exceptionalism
gist: Historian Eva Payne traces how U.S. foreign policy, from colonial-era assimilation campaigns through the WWI-era 'American Plan' and the 2001 Trafficking in Persons Report, exported an American ideal of sexual purity — enforced almost exclusively on women — that still shapes global anti-prostitution law today, even as sex workers from Brazil to Belgium organize against it and Trump-era America simultaneously disengages internationally while intensifying domestic reproductive-rights rollbacks.

insights.claim: U.S. Indian-affairs agents used the 1880s assimilation policy to target Native American women for having sex outside marriage, cohabiting with lovers, or becoming plural wives, treating sexual conduct as a distinct and high-priority category for 'civilizing' tribes even though banning these practices upended Native social structures.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Shows the sexual-morality-as-policy-tool pattern predates and structurally resembles later U.S. foreign interventions — a template for tracing the same logic forward into modern trafficking diplomacy.
insights.quote: government agents insisted that sex was a separate—and highly important—category of conduct and were obsessed with reforming it.
insights.locator: As part of the assimilationist policies of the 1880s

insights.claim: After the Spanish-American War, U.S. officials in the Philippines licensed brothels and required sex workers to submit to weekly venereal-disease exams to keep soldiers 'safe,' even as the same policy left the women themselves unprotected from infection.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Establishes the recurring gap between official U.S. rhetoric and on-the-ground practice around regulating (versus prohibiting) sex work — a credibility gap that resurfaces in the piece's modern critique of U.S.-led anti-trafficking policy.
insights.quote: U.S. leaders believed this policy would make sex safe for soldiers, even as they showed distinctly less concern for the women who remained at risk of infection.
insights.locator: When U.S. soldiers arrived in the Philippines

insights.claim: President Theodore Roosevelt's 1902 demand that the military 'diminish the vice' of prostitution in U.S. colonies changed the rhetoric but not the practice — military officials simply learned to conceal prostitution rather than eliminate it.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: A recurring pattern — moral crackdowns that reshape optics without changing underlying practice — that recurs later in the piece's account of U.S. base towns and modern anti-trafficking enforcement.
insights.quote: It is criminal folly to believe that sexual indulgence is necessary to health,

insights.claim: Funded by John D. Rockefeller Jr., the American Social Hygiene Association's eradicationist arguments in the 1910s fed directly into WWI-era U.S. legislation that made selling sex near a military camp a federal crime and criminalized extramarital sex in nearly every state — a policy reformers branded the 'American Plan' to distinguish it from Europe's regulatory approach.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific philanthropic-to-legislative pipeline that turned a moral crusade into federal law — a template for how private funding repeatedly shaped U.S. sex/vice policy.
insights.quote: the “American Plan” to stress the unique approach of prohibiting prostitution rather than regulating it, as the unsavory Europeans did.
insights.locator: They eventually termed this policy the American Plan

insights.claim: In WWI France, U.S. military police forcibly closed brothels favored by American troops and subjected French women — including Aline Legros, accused by a soldier of infecting him despite a negative test — to compulsory vaginal exams, prompting a French doctor to remark that the two nations could not compare their 'mentality and genital habits.'
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: A concrete case showing U.S. sexual moralism overriding an ally's sovereignty — the piece's clearest illustration of 'soft power' curdling into coercive intervention, a dynamic echoed later in U.S. aid conditionality.
insights.quote: we would not know how to compare the mentality and the genital habits of the two.

insights.claim: Rockefeller-funded U.S. reformers' 1920s-30s investigations defined 'trafficking' as essentially all commercial sex and pushed for criminalizing sex workers while largely ignoring clients; the League of Nations adopted this framing, and the postwar United Nations adopted the League's anti-trafficking conventions verbatim — meaning the century-old American definition of trafficking still underpins today's dominant international and UN policy.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: The core causal chain of the piece: a specific 1920s American ideological framework, not a neutral international consensus, still drives global anti-trafficking law — relevant for any policy analysis of UN trafficking frameworks.
insights.quote: Americans managed to set the terms of international trafficking policy so that it mirrored U.S. policy.
insights.locator: In this way, Americans managed to set the terms

insights.claim: Since 2001, the State Department's annual Trafficking in Persons Report ranks countries by anti-trafficking compliance and threatens aid suspension for noncompliance, rewarding criminalization of commercial sex over poverty-reduction or labor-rights measures that sex workers themselves say would reduce their vulnerability — pressure that has pushed several Asian countries to criminalize prostitution.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: A live policy lever: a country's TIP Report ranking and associated aid risk is a concrete, trackable input for country-risk assessments in Asia and elsewhere.
insights.locator: In 2001, the State Department began to issue

insights.claim: The 2003 Bush-era 'anti-prostitution pledge' cut U.S. funding to NGOs that in any way support legalizing sex work, and tied HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria funding to organizations explicitly opposing prostitution — conditionality so costly that in 2005 Brazil's sexually transmitted disease commissioner rejected $40 million in U.S. funding rather than exclude sex workers as prevention partners.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Quantifies the real financial cost of U.S. moral conditionality on global health funding — a concrete precedent for how ideologically conditioned U.S. aid programs can be rejected outright by recipient governments.
insights.quote: How could we ask prostitutes to take a position against themselves?
insights.locator: In 2005, the Brazilian sexually transmitted disease commissioner

themes.theme: Global pushback and a divided America

insights.claim: Sex workers are now organizing globally against the U.S.-set prohibition template — Belgium recently achieved full decriminalization, letting sex workers unionize and access pensions, parental leave, and healthcare — a divergence the author suggests will accelerate as declining U.S. global influence weakens Washington's grip on the international bodies that set trafficking policy.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Flags a plausible policy-divergence trend (decriminalization gaining ground outside U.S. influence) relevant to labor-rights and public-health policy analysts tracking the space.
insights.locator: In Belgium, for example, sex workers recently achieved

insights.claim: Even as the Trump administration appears less invested in shaping international sexual-morality policy — its claim to global sexual leadership undercut by a president who has boasted about grabbing women 'by the pussy' — it has been domestically aggressive in rolling back sexual, bodily, and reproductive rights, making the U.S. exceptional among Western democracies in the opposite direction from its historic posture.
insights.speaker: Eva Payne
insights.so_what: Identifies a reversal worth tracking: U.S. moral leverage over international sex/trafficking policy may be waning even as domestic reproductive-rights policy tightens — two diverging vectors with different downstream constituencies and legal-risk profiles.
insights.locator: particularly under a president who has boasted
